title: Combine Two Tables
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal database challenge: the single SQL LEFT JOIN solution is also the speed extreme and memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.sql is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The task is a relational projection with optional address rows. SQL executes the join inside the database engine, preserves native NULL semantics, and avoids client-side materialization; this is the fastest and leanest accepted interface for a LeetCode Database problem.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Not a LeetCode Database submission language for this schema; reimplementing the join client-side would require extracting and materializing both tables.
Unsupported for this database prompt and lower-level row handling would add transfer and memory overhead.
Strong for native joins, but unsupported here and dominated by executing the relational operator where the data resides.
Unsupported for this SQL schema submission and would need application-side materialization.
Not the accepted query interface for the database track; managed-runtime joins would allocate more than the SQL projection.
Python/Pandas can express a left merge but materializes DataFrames; the other scripting languages are not the intended database submission path. SQL is leaner and optimizer-driven.

variants.approachJustification: Use Person as the preserved table and left-join Address on personId, projecting only firstName, lastName, city, and state.
variants.timeComplexity: O(P + A) logical join work with a hash/merge plan; O(P log A) with index lookups
variants.spaceComplexity: O(R) output; engine workspace depends on the physical join plan

reasoningSummary: Every Person row must be preserved, while Address is optional. A LEFT JOIN on personId is the direct relational expression; SQL naturally returns NULL for missing right-side columns and ignores orphan Address rows.
timeComplexity: O(P + A) logical join work with optimizer-dependent physical plan
spaceComplexity: O(R) output plus optimizer-dependent join workspace
